texts.orig: SO2/NO2 Separation Driven by NO2 Dimerization on SSZ-13 Zeolite Membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ABSTRACT: The molecular state is crucial for precise gas separation using a zeolite membrane, yet the state control remains a big challenge. Herein, we report a NO 2 dimerization facilitated high performance SO2/NO2 separation on a SSZ-13 zeolite membrane. The NO2 dimerization is triggered by temperature and pressure to form N 2 O4 with big molecular size, and N 2 O4 di ff usion into the zeolite pore is inhibited on the basis of size exclusion, leading to high separation selectivity. Consequently, SO 2 rather than NO2 preferentially permeates through the SSZ-13 membrane with a high SO2 permeance of 2 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 and high SO2/ NO2 separation factor of 22, ∼ 50-fold of that measured without dimerization. The dimerization e ff ect for SO2/NO2 separation prevails in other small-pore zeolites such as NaA. This advanced function is revealed through membrane separation using single and mixture gases.
texts.text: ABSTRACT: The molecular state is crucial for precise gas separation using a zeolite membrane, yet the state control remains a big challenge. Herein, we report a NO 2 dimerization facilitated high performance SO2/NO2 separation on a SSZ-13 zeolite membrane. The NO2 dimerization is triggered by temperature and pressure to form N 2 O4 with big molecular size, and N 2 O4 di ff usion into the zeolite pore is inhibited on the basis of size exclusion, leading to high separation selectivity. Consequently, SO 2 rather than NO2 preferentially permeates through the SSZ-13 membrane with a high SO2 permeance of 2 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 and high SO2/ NO2 separation factor of 22, ∼ 50-fold of that measured without dimerization. The dimerization e ff ect for SO2/NO2 separation prevails in other small-pore zeolites such as NaA. This advanced function is revealed through membrane separation using single and mixture gases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: S igni fi cant industrial emission of SO 2 and NO2 gases needs further treatment before discharging to the atmosphere. 1 End of pipe capture of SO 2 /NO2 is one of the feasible ways for desulfurization/denitri fi cation to reduce their deleterious impact on the environment. 2,3 The capture process generally enriches fl ue gas ( ≤ 0.2% SO2 and NO x in steel, cements, and glass plants) into a concentrated stream of SO2 and NO2 ( ≥ 2%). 4 Pure SO2 and NO2 are pro fi table feedstock (market prices: ∼ $0.36 kg -1 for SO2), ∼ $6.0 kg -1 for NO2) for production of various chemicals (i.e., preservatives, bleaching agent, fungicides/disinfectants). 4,5 Therefore, an e ff ective separation technology is in high demand to receive highpurity SO2 and NO2 individually for further uses.
texts.text: S igni fi cant industrial emission of SO 2 and NO2 gases needs further treatment before discharging to the atmosphere. 1 End of pipe capture of SO 2 /NO2 is one of the feasible ways for desulfurization/denitri fi cation to reduce their deleterious impact on the environment. 2,3 The capture process generally enriches fl ue gas ( ≤ 0.2% SO2 and NO x in steel, cements, and glass plants) into a concentrated stream of SO2 and NO2 ( ≥ 2%). 4 Pure SO2 and NO2 are pro fi table feedstock (market prices: ∼ $0.36 kg -1 for SO2), ∼ $6.0 kg -1 for NO2) for production of various chemicals (i.e., preservatives, bleaching agent, fungicides/disinfectants). 4,5 Therefore, an e ff ective separation technology is in high demand to receive highpurity SO2 and NO2 individually for further uses.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Conventional methods of cryogenic distillation and physical adsorption work for separation of some common gases; however, they are not applicable for SO2/NO2. For cryogenic distillation based upon boiling point di ff erence, a required low temperature can cause NO2 reaction with SO2, 6 meaning that NO2 is unable to be separated from SO2. For physical adsorption, highly polar SO2 and NO2 are both strongly adsorbed on adsorbents, 1,7 leading to poor gas selectivity and adsorbent regeneration di ffi culty. 8
texts.text: Conventional methods of cryogenic distillation and physical adsorption work for separation of some common gases; however, they are not applicable for SO2/NO2. For cryogenic distillation based upon boiling point di ff erence, a required low temperature can cause NO2 reaction with SO2, 6 meaning that NO2 is unable to be separated from SO2. For physical adsorption, highly polar SO2 and NO2 are both strongly adsorbed on adsorbents, 1,7 leading to poor gas selectivity and adsorbent regeneration di ffi culty. 8

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Alternatively, membrane gas separation 9 can be operated continuously in a wide range of temperature and with high selectivity. Zeolite membranes possess molecular-sized pores and tunable surface properties, 10 applicable for gas separations. 11 Previous separations follow either kinetic or thermodynamic mechanisms: (1) small molecules di ff use faster than big ones; thus small-pore zeolite membranes are capable for permeation of small gases, e.g., RUB-15 with six-membered ring for H2/N2 separation, 12 NaA with 4.1 Å pores for O2/N2 separation, 13 and SAPO-34 with 3.8 Å pores for Kr/Xe separation. 14 (2) Functionalized zeolites can adsorb speci fi c gases, allowing the preferential adsorption for permeate gases over others, e.g., ZSM-5 with polar groups for CO2/H2
texts.text: Alternatively, membrane gas separation 9 can be operated continuously in a wide range of temperature and with high selectivity. Zeolite membranes possess molecular-sized pores and tunable surface properties, 10 applicable for gas separations. 11 Previous separations follow either kinetic or thermodynamic mechanisms: (1) small molecules di ff use faster than big ones; thus small-pore zeolite membranes are capable for permeation of small gases, e.g., RUB-15 with six-membered ring for H2/N2 separation, 12 NaA with 4.1 Å pores for O2/N2 separation, 13 and SAPO-34 with 3.8 Å pores for Kr/Xe separation. 14 (2) Functionalized zeolites can adsorb speci fi c gases, allowing the preferential adsorption for permeate gases over others, e.g., ZSM-5 with polar groups for CO2/H2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: separation 15 and Ag-exchanged X zeolite for C2H4/C2H6 separation. 16 However, the above classical mechanisms remain a grand challenge for SO2/NO2 separation using zeolite membranes (Figure 1a) due to the following contradicted e ff ects: the smaller kinetic diameter of NO 2 (3.3 Å) over SO2 (4.1 Å) 17 allows fast di ff usion of NO2 rather than SO2. Conversely, the relatively higher polarizability and dipole moment of SO2 (42.80 × 10 -25 cm 3 , 1.63 × 10 -18 ESU cm) compared to NO2 (30.20 × 10 -25 cm 3 , 0.316 × 10 -18 ESU cm) 18 endow SO2 adsorption a ffi nity, enabling preferential transport of SO2 over NO2.
texts.text: separation 15 and Ag-exchanged X zeolite for C2H4/C2H6 separation. 16 However, the above classical mechanisms remain a grand challenge for SO2/NO2 separation using zeolite membranes (Figure 1a) due to the following contradicted e ff ects: the smaller kinetic diameter of NO 2 (3.3 Å) over SO2 (4.1 Å) 17 allows fast di ff usion of NO2 rather than SO2. Conversely, the relatively higher polarizability and dipole moment of SO2 (42.80 × 10 -25 cm 3 , 1.63 × 10 -18 ESU cm) compared to NO2 (30.20 × 10 -25 cm 3 , 0.316 × 10 -18 ESU cm) 18 endow SO2 adsorption a ffi nity, enabling preferential transport of SO2 over NO2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Herein, we present an exceptional approach named as dimerization-driven separation (Figure 1a) for selective membrane permeation of SO2 over NO2. In principle, NO2 is likely to dimerize into N2O4 at low temperature or high pressure, 19 and N2O4 is larger than SO2 (Bondi molecular size: 20 4.6 Å for N2O4 versus 4.3 Å for SO2). Upon NO2 dimerization, SO2 has syntropic contributions of smaller size and higher polarizability than N2O4, theoretically facilitating SO2 penetration through the membrane. To validate this hypothesis, a SSZ-13 (CHA type) zeolite membrane was synthesized for SO2/NO2 separation. SSZ-13 has elliptical pores of 3.7 × 4.2 Å (Figure 1b), 21 ideal for molecular sieving of SO2 over N2O4. SSZ-13 can be formed in high silica, 22 rendering its good chemical stability (i.e., acid resistance), applicable for gas separation from fl ue gas.
texts.text: Herein, we present an exceptional approach named as dimerization-driven separation (Figure 1a) for selective membrane permeation of SO2 over NO2. In principle, NO2 is likely to dimerize into N2O4 at low temperature or high pressure, 19 and N2O4 is larger than SO2 (Bondi molecular size: 20 4.6 Å for N2O4 versus 4.3 Å for SO2). Upon NO2 dimerization, SO2 has syntropic contributions of smaller size and higher polarizability than N2O4, theoretically facilitating SO2 penetration through the membrane. To validate this hypothesis, a SSZ-13 (CHA type) zeolite membrane was synthesized for SO2/NO2 separation. SSZ-13 has elliptical pores of 3.7 × 4.2 Å (Figure 1b), 21 ideal for molecular sieving of SO2 over N2O4. SSZ-13 can be formed in high silica, 22 rendering its good chemical stability (i.e., acid resistance), applicable for gas separation from fl ue gas.

texts.orig: Figure 1. Schematics of (a) classical and dimerization-driven gas separations, (b) CHA zeolite and kinetic/Bondi molecular sizes (SO 2 , NO2, N2O4), (c) top and (d) cross-sectional SEM images, and (e) XRD pattern of the SSZ-13 membrane.
texts.text: Figure 1. Schematics of (a) classical and dimerization-driven gas separations, (b) CHA zeolite and kinetic/Bondi molecular sizes (SO 2 , NO2, N2O4), (c) top and (d) cross-sectional SEM images, and (e) XRD pattern of the SSZ-13 membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The SSZ-13 membrane was synthesized by seeded growth on a porous α -Al2O3 support. The XRD pattern (Figure 1e) contains a set of distinct di ff raction peaks, characteristic of the CHA topological zeolite. Regular fringes and discrete spots (HRTEM and SAED, Figure S4) indicate the pure-phase crystalline membrane. The top SEM image (Figure 1c) shows that the micrometer-sized crystals (2 -4 μ m) are intergrown well and free of visible cracks, revealing high continuity of the membrane. The cross-sectional SEM image (Figure 1d) shows a crystal-comparable membrane thickness of ∼ 2.0 μ m. SSZ-13 crystals collected from the membrane have Si/Al ratio of 37 and surface area of 557 m 2 g -1 (Figure S8). The measured pore size of 4 Å agrees with the SSZ-13 crystallographic value. A cuto ff in gas permeances between probe molecules (N2, 3.64 Å; C2H6, 4.4 Å) (Figure S9) supplements that the membrane mainly contains zeolite pores.
texts.text: The SSZ-13 membrane was synthesized by seeded growth on a porous α -Al2O3 support. The XRD pattern (Figure 1e) contains a set of distinct di ff raction peaks, characteristic of the CHA topological zeolite. Regular fringes and discrete spots (HRTEM and SAED, Figure S4) indicate the pure-phase crystalline membrane. The top SEM image (Figure 1c) shows that the micrometer-sized crystals (2 -4 μ m) are intergrown well and free of visible cracks, revealing high continuity of the membrane. The cross-sectional SEM image (Figure 1d) shows a crystal-comparable membrane thickness of ∼ 2.0 μ m. SSZ-13 crystals collected from the membrane have Si/Al ratio of 37 and surface area of 557 m 2 g -1 (Figure S8). The measured pore size of 4 Å agrees with the SSZ-13 crystallographic value. A cuto ff in gas permeances between probe molecules (N2, 3.64 Å; C2H6, 4.4 Å) (Figure S9) supplements that the membrane mainly contains zeolite pores.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The proof of concept for dimerization-driven separation was demonstrated by SO2/NO2 permeations through the SSZ-13 membrane. Figure 2a shows the permeation data of SO2 and NO2 unary gases at 368, 323, and 288 K and at a total feed pressure of 0.15 MPa (5% unary gas balanced by N2, namely, 5% SO2/95% N2, 5% NO2/95% N2). At 368 K (a typical temperature of fl ue gas), NO 2 and SO2 permeances are 3.68 × 10 -8 and 1.68 × 10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 , respectively, showing

At 368 K (a typical temperature of fl ue gas), NO 2 and SO2 permeances are 3.68 × 10 -8 and 1.68 × 10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 , respectively, showing

texts.orig: Figure 2. Unary gas (5% SO2/95% N2 or 5% NO2/95% N2) permeance and SO2/NO2 ideal SF on the SSZ-13 membrane as a function of (a) temperature at 0.15 MPa and (b) pressure at 288 K.
texts.text: Figure 2. Unary gas (5% SO2/95% N2 or 5% NO2/95% N2) permeance and SO2/NO2 ideal SF on the SSZ-13 membrane as a function of (a) temperature at 0.15 MPa and (b) pressure at 288 K.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: that NO2 is the permeate of priority. At this high temperature, NO2 remains its monomeric status; and small NO2 molecules di ff use faster than big SO2 molecules. Upon reducing the temperature from 368 to 323 K, a reverse gas permeation phenomenon is observed and fi rst reported for gas permeation with zeolite membrane: SO2 instead of NO2 becomes the preferentially permeated gas, shown by the larger permeance of SO2 than NO2. Concurrently, the ideal separation factor (SF) of SO2/NO2 is reversed to 1.2 from 0.45. NO 2 dimerization is the driving force for this reverse: upon dimerization occurrence, NO2 is transformed to N2O4, and N2O4 can hardly penetrate through the zeolite, attributed to the molecule (4.6 Å) being larger than the pore (3.7 × 4.2 Å). With a further temperature decrease to 288 K, SO2 permeance is signi fi cantly increased and overwhelmingly surpasses NO2, resulting in a high SO2/NO2 SF of 22 ( ∼ 50-fold of that at 368 K). The substantial SF enhancement is due to the dimerization-induced SO2 permeation via concerted adsorption and di ff usion, which is supported by a larger isosteric heat of adsorption and a smaller apparent activation energy of SO2 over NO2 (Table S2).
texts.text: that NO2 is the permeate of priority. At this high temperature, NO2 remains its monomeric status; and small NO2 molecules di ff use faster than big SO2 molecules. Upon reducing the temperature from 368 to 323 K, a reverse gas permeation phenomenon is observed and fi rst reported for gas permeation with zeolite membrane: SO2 instead of NO2 becomes the preferentially permeated gas, shown by the larger permeance of SO2 than NO2. Concurrently, the ideal separation factor (SF) of SO2/NO2 is reversed to 1.2 from 0.45. NO 2 dimerization is the driving force for this reverse: upon dimerization occurrence, NO2 is transformed to N2O4, and N2O4 can hardly penetrate through the zeolite, attributed to the molecule (4.6 Å) being larger than the pore (3.7 × 4.2 Å). With a further temperature decrease to 288 K, SO2 permeance is signi fi cantly increased and overwhelmingly surpasses NO2, resulting in a high SO2/NO2 SF of 22 ( ∼ 50-fold of that at 368 K). The substantial SF enhancement is due to the dimerization-induced SO2 permeation via concerted adsorption and di ff usion, which is supported by a larger isosteric heat of adsorption and a smaller apparent activation energy of SO2 over NO2 (Table S2).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The temperature-dependent dimerization was interpreted by the chemical-equilibrium theory (Figure S11). At 368 K, the equilibrium mole fraction of N2O4 in NO2 -N2O4 is only 0.0064 at the NO2 pressure of 0.0075 MPa (this is the same pressure in the experiment). The N 2 O4 mole fraction increases to 0.074 at 323 K and then further increases to 0.48 at 288 K. The rapid reduction in NO2 mole fraction is consistent with the descending trend of temperature-varied NO2 permeance (Figure 2a).
texts.text: The temperature-dependent dimerization was interpreted by the chemical-equilibrium theory (Figure S11). At 368 K, the equilibrium mole fraction of N2O4 in NO2 -N2O4 is only 0.0064 at the NO2 pressure of 0.0075 MPa (this is the same pressure in the experiment). The N 2 O4 mole fraction increases to 0.074 at 323 K and then further increases to 0.48 at 288 K. The rapid reduction in NO2 mole fraction is consistent with the descending trend of temperature-varied NO2 permeance (Figure 2a).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pressure was used to trigger the dimerization of NO2 to N2O4. Figure 2b shows pressure-varied SO2 and NO2 permeances and SO2/NO2 ideal SFs at 288 K. The SO2 permeance declines with increasing feed pressure, ascribed to the nonlinear SO2 adsorption in SSZ-13 (Figure S12a). SO2/ NO2 SFs remain almost constant at high pressures of 0.2 -0.35 MPa (Figure 2b), implying that NO2 permeance experiences a reduction in identical amplitude to SO 2 . This fi nding disagrees with the adsorption trend that linear NO 2 adsorption (Figure S12b) principally gives no permeance decrease. As we know, the dimerization continuously produces N2O4 and a reduction of NO2 in quantity occurs simultaneously. It can be evidenced by a 25% decline in NO2 mole fraction (from 0.52 to 0.39) from 0.15 to 0.35 MPa for total pressure (accordingly, NO2 partial pressures from 0.0075 to 0.0175 MPa). The dimerization makes an additional contribution in reduction of NO2 permeance, consequently to maintain the SF value.
texts.text: Pressure was used to trigger the dimerization of NO2 to N2O4. Figure 2b shows pressure-varied SO2 and NO2 permeances and SO2/NO2 ideal SFs at 288 K. The SO2 permeance declines with increasing feed pressure, ascribed to the nonlinear SO2 adsorption in SSZ-13 (Figure S12a). SO2/ NO2 SFs remain almost constant at high pressures of 0.2 -0.35 MPa (Figure 2b), implying that NO2 permeance experiences a reduction in identical amplitude to SO 2 . This fi nding disagrees with the adsorption trend that linear NO 2 adsorption (Figure S12b) principally gives no permeance decrease. As we know, the dimerization continuously produces N2O4 and a reduction of NO2 in quantity occurs simultaneously. It can be evidenced by a 25% decline in NO2 mole fraction (from 0.52 to 0.39) from 0.15 to 0.35 MPa for total pressure (accordingly, NO2 partial pressures from 0.0075 to 0.0175 MPa). The dimerization makes an additional contribution in reduction of NO2 permeance, consequently to maintain the SF value.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To understand gas separation at microlevel, molecular states of NO2 were investigated by IR (DRIFTS) and EPR characterizations and DFT and molecular dynamics calculations. IR spectra of the SSZ-13 membrane exposed to NO2 fl ow (Figure 3a) show that the intensities of N x O y adsorbed species evolve distinctively with exposure time. The spectra remain constant after 20 min in the entire wavenumber range, indicating that the membrane is saturated with N x O y adsorption (Figure S14). The bands corresponding to symmetric (sym-N2O4) and asymmetric (cis-/trans-N2O4) isomers of N2O4 appear at 1754 and 2600 cm -1 , respectively. 23 -25 Other bands are distinguished at 1810, 1190 -1595, and 1105 cm -1 which are assigned to NO + , NO3 -, and NO2 -respectively (Table S6). The NO + -NO3 -pair is formed via N2O4 disproportionation in zeolite pores: NO3 -presumably coordinates with Si -O -Al and sodium sites to
texts.text: To understand gas separation at microlevel, molecular states of NO2 were investigated by IR (DRIFTS) and EPR characterizations and DFT and molecular dynamics calculations. IR spectra of the SSZ-13 membrane exposed to NO2 fl ow (Figure 3a) show that the intensities of N x O y adsorbed species evolve distinctively with exposure time. The spectra remain constant after 20 min in the entire wavenumber range, indicating that the membrane is saturated with N x O y adsorption (Figure S14). The bands corresponding to symmetric (sym-N2O4) and asymmetric (cis-/trans-N2O4) isomers of N2O4 appear at 1754 and 2600 cm -1 , respectively. 23 -25 Other bands are distinguished at 1810, 1190 -1595, and 1105 cm -1 which are assigned to NO + , NO3 -, and NO2 -respectively (Table S6). texts.label: text
texts.orig: form monodentate, bidentate, bridged, and Na + -localized con fi gurations. 25 -27 NO + tends to bind with framework oxygen (O z ), and NO2 -could be yielded from NO + -O z binding. 28 The concentration ratio between sym-N2O4, cisN2O4, trans-N2O4, and NO + NO3 -is quanti fi ed (Figures S16 and S17) to be 22:157:203:1, indicating that N2O4 is the predominant adsorbed species. The EPR signal of NO2adsorbed SSZ-13 (Figure S15) becomes weaker with temperature decrease and even disappears at 200 K, suggesting that paramagnetic NO2 is gradually dimerized into diamagnetic N2O4.
texts.text: form monodentate, bidentate, bridged, and Na + -localized con fi gurations. 25 -27 NO + tends to bind with framework oxygen (O z ), and NO2 -could be yielded from NO + -O z binding. 28 The concentration ratio between sym-N2O4, cisN2O4, trans-N2O4, and NO + NO3 -is quanti fi ed (Figures S16 and S17) to be 22:157:203:1, indicating that N2O4 is the predominant adsorbed species. The EPR signal of NO2adsorbed SSZ-13 (Figure S15) becomes weaker with temperature decrease and even disappears at 200 K, suggesting that paramagnetic NO2 is gradually dimerized into diamagnetic N2O4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: N2O4 molecule has three isomeric con fi gurations of symN2O4, cis-N2O4, and trans-N2O4. If sym-N2O4 or cis-N2O4 is approaching the SSZ-13 pore and going to be adsorbed, the adsorption enthalpy ( Δ E ) is positive (Figure 3b), implying that sym- and cis-N 2 O4 can only be adsorbed on the external surface rather than internal pore, and thus the membrane is impermeable to both isomers. The negative Δ E and stepwise decrease in enthalpy show that trans-N 2 O4 can be adsorbed in the pore and some transient species could be disproportionated to NO + and NO3 -through van der Waals interaction between con fi ned N2O4 and pore wall. 29,30 NO + and NO3 -fi rmly sit around the pore window, thus preventing the entrance of gaseous NO2 and N2O4 into the pore. The density distributions of SO2, NO2, and N2O4 in the mixture gas di ff usion across SSZ-13 (Figure 3c) show that SO2 is mainly located in the membrane center and at the permeate side, in contrast to N2O4 that only resides at the feed side. The nondimerized NO2 is also able to transport through the membrane. The above results validate that NO2 dimerization to N2O4 indeed takes place and N2O4 permeation is excluded by SSZ-13 via surface obstruction or pore blockage.
texts.text: N2O4 molecule has three isomeric con fi gurations of symN2O4, cis-N2O4, and trans-N2O4. If sym-N2O4 or cis-N2O4 is approaching the SSZ-13 pore and going to be adsorbed, the adsorption enthalpy ( Δ E ) is positive (Figure 3b), implying that sym- and cis-N 2 O4 can only be adsorbed on the external surface rather than internal pore, and thus the membrane is impermeable to both isomers. The negative Δ E and stepwise decrease in enthalpy show that trans-N 2 O4 can be adsorbed in the pore and some transient species could be disproportionated to NO + and NO3 -through van der Waals interaction between con fi ned N2O4 and pore wall. 29,30 NO + and NO3 -fi rmly sit around the pore window, thus preventing the entrance of gaseous NO2 and N2O4 into the pore. The density distributions of SO2, NO2, and N2O4 in the mixture gas di ff usion across SSZ-13 (Figure 3c) show that SO2 is mainly located in the membrane center and at the permeate side, in contrast to N2O4 that only resides at the feed side. The nondimerized NO2 is also able to transport through the membrane. The above results validate that NO2 dimerization to N2O4 indeed takes place and N2O4 permeation is excluded by SSZ-13 via surface obstruction or pore blockage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To extend the dimerization-driven separation in other zeolite membranes, NaA (Figures S2 and S6) with identical eight-membered-ring pores to SSZ-13 was selected and unary gas permeation of SO2 or NO2 was conducted (Figure 4a).
texts.text: To extend the dimerization-driven separation in other zeolite membranes, NaA (Figures S2 and S6) with identical eight-membered-ring pores to SSZ-13 was selected and unary gas permeation of SO2 or NO2 was conducted (Figure 4a).

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 4. (a) Temperature-dependent unary gas permeations of SO2 and NO2 on NaA and ZSM-5 membranes; SO2/NO2 binary mixture (5% SO2/5% NO2/90% N2) separation on the SSZ-13 membrane as a function of (b) temperature (at 0.15 MPa), (c) pressure (at 288 K), and (d) time (0.15 MPa, 288 K).
texts.text: Figure 4. (a) Temperature-dependent unary gas permeations of SO2 and NO2 on NaA and ZSM-5 membranes; SO2/NO2 binary mixture (5% SO2/5% NO2/90% N2) separation on the SSZ-13 membrane as a function of (b) temperature (at 0.15 MPa), (c) pressure (at 288 K), and (d) time (0.15 MPa, 288 K).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Expectably, the SO2 /NO2 ideal SF is increased from 0.8 to 7.6 with decreasing temperature from 368 to 288 K. This strengthened e ff ect of NO2 dimerization is also observed in the pressure-dependent experiment (Figure S21). In contrast, ZSM-5 (Figures S3 and S7) with 10-membered-ring pores (5.5 Å), larger than any species of NO2, SO2, and N2O4, has no capability in separating SO2 from NO2, evidenced by SF of ∼ 1.0 regardless of temperature (Figure 4a) and pressure (Figure S22). The above results verify the universality of dimerization-driven SO2 /NO2 separation by small-pore zeolite membranes which have critical pore sizes between SO2 and N2O4 molecules.
texts.text: Expectably, the SO2 /NO2 ideal SF is increased from 0.8 to 7.6 with decreasing temperature from 368 to 288 K. This strengthened e ff ect of NO2 dimerization is also observed in the pressure-dependent experiment (Figure S21). In contrast, ZSM-5 (Figures S3 and S7) with 10-membered-ring pores (5.5 Å), larger than any species of NO2, SO2, and N2O4, has no capability in separating SO2 from NO2, evidenced by SF of ∼ 1.0 regardless of temperature (Figure 4a) and pressure (Figure S22). The above results verify the universality of dimerization-driven SO2 /NO2 separation by small-pore zeolite membranes which have critical pore sizes between SO2 and N2O4 molecules.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For separation of SO2/NO2 binary mixture (5% SO2/5% NO2, balanced by 90% N2), 4 temperatureand pressuredependent gas permeance and separation factor pro fi les (Figure 4b,c) resemble those of unary gases (Figure 2). The SO2/NO2 SFs are slightly lower than the ideal ones, due to the competitive adsorption and di ff usion between SO2 and NO2 since SO2 coexists with both NO2 and N2O4 (NO2:N2O4 = 0.52:0.48 in mole fraction at 288 K and 0.15 MPa). During life (Figure 4d) and repetition (Table S3) tests, the preservation of SO2/NO2 SF ( ∼ 16) and SO2 permeance ( ∼ 4.8 × 10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 ) indicates the stability and reproducibility of the membrane. SO2/NO2 SFs of >15.2 are obtained when CO2, O2, and NO are present in the gas stream (Table S4), and an operation cost is estimated at $0.06/m 3 gas , STP (Table S8), comparable to conventional membrane separation (e.g., H2/ CO2 separation 31 ), suggesting the membrane practicability.
texts.text: For separation of SO2/NO2 binary mixture (5% SO2/5% NO2, balanced by 90% N2), 4 temperatureand pressuredependent gas permeance and separation factor pro fi les (Figure 4b,c) resemble those of unary gases (Figure 2). The SO2/NO2 SFs are slightly lower than the ideal ones, due to the competitive adsorption and di ff usion between SO2 and NO2 since SO2 coexists with both NO2 and N2O4 (NO2:N2O4 = 0.52:0.48 in mole fraction at 288 K and 0.15 MPa). During life (Figure 4d) and repetition (Table S3) tests, the preservation of SO2/NO2 SF ( ∼ 16) and SO2 permeance ( ∼ 4.8 × 10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 ) indicates the stability and reproducibility of the membrane. SO2/NO2 SFs of >15.2 are obtained when CO2, O2, and NO are present in the gas stream (Table S4), and an operation cost is estimated at $0.06/m 3 gas , STP (Table S8), comparable to conventional membrane separation (e.g., H2/ CO2 separation 31 ), suggesting the membrane practicability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In summary, selective separation of SO2 from NO2 was realized through a dimerization-driven approach on small-pore zeolite membranes. NO2 dimerization took place upon

texts.orig: temperature decrease or pressure increase; as a consequence, N2O4 molecules were formed and did not enter zeolite pores due to the size exclusion. SO 2 preferentially permeated through the membrane during the dimerization, giving a high SO2/NO2 separation factor. This study demonstrates a nonclassical separation process on zeolite membranes, which will enrich membrane science.
